coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots, the carbon nanomaterials, have been greatly developed for several applications including fluorescent sensors for metal ion detection, antioxidant activity, and plant growth promotion due to their excellent photophysical properties, non-toxicity, and high quantum yield. Therefore, this research focused on the synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots from elephant manure via green hydrothermal synthesis owing to its chemical composition of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. The as-synthesized carbon dots exhibited blue fluorescence emission under UV light and the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence enhancement. Carbon dots could be further used as a “turn-off” fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ detection as a part of environmental monitoring. Furthermore, the as-synthesized carbon dots containing high phenolic content showed the antioxidant activity and promoted rice growth with an increase in the characteristic aroma compound (2-acetyl-1-pyrroline), confirmed by GC–MS analysis.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of elephant manure-derived carbon dots and multifunctional applications: Metal sensing, antioxidant, and rice plant promotion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots, the carbon nanomaterials, have been greatly developed for several applications including fluorescent sensors for metal ion detection, antioxidant activity, and plant growth promotion due to their excellent photophysical properties, non-toxicity, and high quantum yield. Therefore, this research focused on the synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots from elephant manure via green hydrothermal synthesis owing to its chemical composition of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. The as-synthesized carbon dots exhibited blue fluorescence emission under UV light and the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence enhancement. Carbon dots could be further used as a “turn-off” fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ detection as a part of environmental monitoring. Furthermore, the as-synthesized carbon dots containing high phenolic content showed the antioxidant activity and promoted rice growth with an increase in the characteristic aroma compound (2-acetyl-1-pyrroline), confirmed by GC–MS analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanoparticles that have a quasi-spherical shape and the size of <10 nm [1]. The modifying parameters of CDs including size, shape, composition, internal structure, and surface chemistry can increase photophysical properties of CDs including strong photoluminescence emission and optical properties, high chemical stability, broad excitation wavelength, and tunable emission wavelength, environmental friendliness, nontoxicity, and resistance to photobleaching [2–5]. Several properties of CDs can promote the plant-growth in the pre-harvest process [6]. For example, the surface hydrophilic groups of CDs promote the seed germination. High nitrogen contents (>60%) could be applied as the nano-fertilizer. Blue and red fluorescence emission of CDs with the electron donor and acceptors increased photosynthesis. Furthermore, the antioxidant of CDs can increase the stress tolerance of plants. Recently, CDs synthesized from Spirulina as biostimulants enhanced the growth of lentil seedlings [7]. Moreover, the excellent photophysical properties have made CDs become a promising candidate for fluorescent sensors to monitor toxic metal ions such as Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+. Mercury (Hg) as a type of heavy metals is poisonous despite being used at low concentrations, resulting in human health problems and environmental pollution [8,9]. Contamination of mercury (Hg) compounds: elemental mercury (Hg0), inorganic mercury salts, and organic mercury compounds, results in Minamata disease or mercury poisoning [10–12]. Iron (Fe) is an essential element for biological processes and a transition metal capable of accepting and donating electrons as an oxidant or reductant. Physiologically, iron can form complexes with iron‑sulfur of proteins and enzymes that function in the transport, handling, and utilization of oxygen, such as hemoglobin, myoglobin, DNA synthesis, and tissue repair [13]. The excess free iron in blood and cells leads to the production of free radicals, or reactive oxygen species (ROS), that can cause damage to cells and tissues in various systems in the body and inhibit enzyme activities [14]. As the excellent photophysical properties of CDs synthesized from green precursors such as fruits peels, beverages, animals, flours, vegetables, plant leaves, or waste materials, CDs have been developed as fluorescent sensors for detecting metal ions including Hg2+, Fe3+, or Cu2+ [15,16]. Nonetheless, various fluorescent CDs for sensing these ions exhibited low quantum yield in the range of 2.8%–11.5% for Hg2+, 3.1%–16.4% for Fe3+, and 2.5%–6.2% for Cu2+ [15].

For the CDs preparations, top-down and bottom-up methods can be used to synthesize CDs [17]. Top-down techniques turn larger substances into smaller particles in nanoscale, involving harsh oxidizing agents to provide higher yield of the particles. On the other hand, bottom-up techniques construct a nanoparticle from smaller constituent parts with the controllable size and morphology. A hydrothermal method has been utilized to synthesize CDs as this method only requires high temperature and pressure in aqueous conditions. The method is also cost-effective, easy to operate, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly to users [18]. Biomass waste as a renewable precursor for CDs have sufficient carbon content to be used for the synthesis of CDs due to its environmental friendliness and mass production [19]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to investigate new carbon sources for simple, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly synthesis of fluorescent CDs. Animal waste-derived CDs from crab shell, prawn shell, cow manure, or pigeon feathers and manure have been reported [20]. Among different sources of animal manures, herbivore's manure contains 50%–80% lignocelluloses related to the feed and digestion system of the animal [21]. Therefore, CDs based on cow manure was developed for fluorescence imaging with high cellular selectivity to breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) [22], glucose detection and mitochondrial selective probe for melanoma skin cancer [23]. Among herbivore's manure, elephant manure contains high cellulose content due to the 30%–40% digestion of the feed [21]. It composes of approximately 40 wt% cellulose, 23 wt% lignin, and 10 wt% hemicellulose [21].

In our study, carbon nanodots were synthesized from the elephant manure as abundant biomass precursors via a green hydrothermal method to elevate high-value-added materials, including fluorescent sensors for metal ions and plant growth promotion. The characteristics and photophysical properties of the as-synthesized ECDs were also investigated. The ECDs demonstrated strong absorption peaks in the ultraviolet range due to the π → π⁎ transition and fluorescence emission depended on the excitation with high photostability and no photobleaching. The multifunctional applications of ECDs were also demonstrated such as fluorescent sensors in the solution and paper strip tests for metal ion sensing in real water samples and the promotion of rice growth.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Quinine hemisulfate monohydrate, diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl (DPPH), gallic acid, and Folin-Ciocalteu's reagent were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Saint Louis, MO, USA). Hg(OAc)2 was purchased from QReC (New Zealand). Na2CO3 was purchased from Kemaus (N.S.W., Australia). Pb(OAc)2⋅3H2O, Cu(OAc)2⋅H2O, Zn(OAc)2, Co(OAc)2⋅4H2O, Mg(OAc)2⋅4H2O, Mn(OAc)2 and FeCl3⋅6H2O were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Cd(OAc)2⋅2H2O, Ca(OAc)2⋅H2O, Fe(OAc)2 and Ni(OAc)2⋅4H2O were purchased from Acros Organics. NaCl was purchased from BDH (Ohio, USA). As2O3 and Na2HAsO4⋅7H2O was purchased from Ajax Finechem. 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline with a purity of 95% was purchased from BOC Sciences (New York, USA). The 0.2-μm filter was purchased from Filtep-Bio. A dialysis membrane tubing (1000 Da) was purchased from Spectra/Por (USA). Deionized water (R > 18 M Ω cm−1) was used in all experiments.

The zeta potentials and particle sizes were analyzed using a Malvern Zetasizer Ultra (Malvern Panalytical, United Kingdom). Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet is5/iD7 ATR spectrometer in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 (ThermoFisher Scientific, MA, USA). pH values were recorded on a Denver UltraBasic model UB-10 Benchtop pH Meter (IL, United States). UV–Vis absorption spectra were performed on a JASCO V-570 UV/Visible spectrometer (Easton, United States). Fluorescence emission spectra were collected on JASCO FP-8500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Easton, United States). The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was collected on the Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed on a X'Pert PRO MPD model PW 3040/60 diffractometer (Panalytical, United Kingdom).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations of elephant manure-derived carbon dots (ECDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The ECDs including ECDs-6 h, ECDs-12 h, and ECDs-24 h were synthesized from dried elephant manure via a hydrothermal method at 200°C for 6, 12, and 24 h, respectively. The fresh elephant manure was obtained from in Ayutthaya Elephant Palace & Royal Kraal, Ayutthaya province, Thailand. The elephant manure was kept at −20°C. The elephant manure was dried at 80°C in a hot air oven for 72 h. The brown powder with the diameter <74 μm was prepared by blending and then collecting by 200 mesh stainless steel sieve. The ECDs was prepared by the mixture of the dried power (2.0 g) in deionized water (50.0 mL) with ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (150 mL). After the hydrothermal reactor was heated at 200°C for 6–24 h, the reaction mixture was cooled down to room temperature. The orange-brown suspension of ECDs was filtrated from the reaction mixture using filter paper No.1 and 0.2 μm-membrane filter, respectively. Finally, the resulting solution was dialyzed in deionized water via dialysis membrane tubing (1000 Da) for 24 h, followed by freeze drying to provide a brown solid in a 48.2 ± 5.8 mg (2.4% yield), 25.6 ± 0.1 mg (1.3% yield), and 24.1 ± 7.1 mg (1.2% yield) of ECDs-6 h, ECDs-12 h, and ECDs-24 h, respectively.

The fluorescence quantum yields (Φ) of ECDs were measured in deionized water by comparing quinine sulfate monohydrate (Φ = 0.54) in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a standard reference according to the literature [24]. The quantum yields of ECDs-6 h, ECDs-12 h, and ECDs-24 h were explored in the concentration ranges of 0.01–0.05 mg/mL, determined by Eq. 1:

The subscript ST refers to the standard reference and X for the sample reference. Slope is the slope from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance. η denotes the refractive index of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence emission of ECDs (0.5 mg/mL) for diverse metal ions (1.0 mM) including Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, As3+, As5+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, and Hg2+ was elucidated in deionized water by excitation of 360 nm. For the interference ion experiment, the fluorescence quenching of ECDs (0.5 mg/mL) in the presence of Hg2+, Fe3+, and Fe2+ ions with other metal ions (1.0 mM) was measured.

The fluorescence titration experiments of ECDs were investigated by increasing concentrations in the range of 0–30 mM for Hg2+, 0–8 mM for Fe3+, and 0–9 mM for Fe2+. The limit of detection (LOD) was investigated from the slope (m) of a linear relationship plot of fluorescence quenching intensity and concentrations of metal ions. The standard derivation (σ) was determined by the fluorescence intensity of the ECDs solution. The detection limit was calculated from Eq. 2:
(2)

The paper test strips based ECDs were prepared by dipping the filter paper (No.1, Whatmann) in the solution of ECDs (1.0 mg/mL) for 5 min. After drying at room temperature, the ECDs coated paper test strips were tested with different metal ions (1.0 mM) including Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, As3+, As5+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, and Hg2+. The fluorescence emission of the paper test strip was observed under UV illumination (365 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ detections in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para:
The drinking water samples spiked with Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ at the concentrations of 30 and 50 μM were determined by fluorescence measurement. All the data were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation with triplicate experiments. The percentage recoveries were calculated as the following Eq. 4:
(4)

Total phenolic contents of ECDs were measured by Folin-Ciocalteu method with slight modification [25]. A standard calibration curve of gallic acid (1–50 μg/L) was used to determine total phenolic content of ECDs as mg of gallic acid equivalent (GAE). The stock solution of gallic acid, 10% Folin-Ciocalteu's reagent, and the ECDs solution (1.0 mg/mL) were prepared in deionized water. The 10% Folin-Ciocalteu's reagent (500 μL) was added to the ECDs solution (50 μL), followed by incubation at room temperature under the dark condition. After 5 min, 5% Na2CO3 (500 μL) was added to the resulting solution and then kept under the dark condition for 2 h. The absorbance at 700 nm was measured using UV–Vis spectrometer.

The antioxidant potential (IC50) of ECDs was evaluated by DPPH radical scavenging method with some modifications [26]. The stock solutions (20 mg/mL) of ECDs were prepared in deionized water, and the stock solution (1.0 mM) of DPPH was prepared in methanol. The DPPH solution (1.0 mM, 250 μL) was mixed with methanol (250 μL). Several concentrations of ECDs in the range of 0–10 mg/mL were then added to the DPPH solution, followed by adjusting the final volume to 1.0 mL with deionized water. The reaction solutions of ECDs in 50% MeOH/H2O were incubated in a dark environment at room temperature for 30 min. The absorbance was measured at 520 nm by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The percent inhibitions of ECDs were calculated as Eq. 5:
(5)
% Inhibition =
A control
−
A sample
A control
× 100

Where Acontrol was the absorbance of the DPPH solution and Asample was the absorbance of the DPPH solution in the addition of the ECDs solution. The IC50 values were calculated using Grafit 5.0 computer software (Erithacus Sofware, Horley, UK).

The rice seeds of Khao Gor Kor 43 were received from the Pathum Thani Rice Research Center and used as a model for plant growth studies. The rice seeds were sterilized with 0.1% v/v NaClO for 30 min and then washed with deionized water, followed by drying at 40°C in a hot air oven until the moisture content was <13%. Several concentrations of the ECDs solution, including 10, 50, and 100 mg/L, were prepared in deionized water. The sterilized rice seeds (25.0 g) were soaked in deionized water as the control experiment and in the different ECDs concentrations (50 mL) for 24 h. The soaked rice seeds were grown on germination paper submerged in distilled water at room temperature. The ECDs solution (50 mL) was sprayed daily until harvested. The rice seedling was harvested at day 10. The rice shoot was separated from the rice root, and the shoot length was then measured.

The 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline (2AP) content of germinated rice shoot in ECDs solutions (0–100 mg/mL) was examined according to the previous literature [27]. The germinated rice shoots were kept at −20°C for 7 days before the 2AP determination. Briefly, the germinated rice shoots (3.0 g) were homogenized using a CryoMill (Retsch, Germany) with liquid nitrogen cooling. The stock solution (0.01 mg/mL) of trimethyl pyridine (TMP) as an internal standard was prepared in ethanol. The homogenized samples (0.3 g) and TMP solution (10 μL) were mixed in ethanol (760 μL) at room temperature. After mixing for 2 h, the supernatant was separated by centrifugation (13,000 rpm, 10 min) and further analyzed by gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GC–MS) analysis. The stock solution (5.0 mg/mL) of 2AP was prepared in 50% methanol/toluene. The calibration curve was prepared from different concentrations of 2AP (0.01–2.5 mg/mL) and TMP solution (10 μL) in ethanol (760 μL). The 2AP content was determined from the calibration curve using GC–MS analysis. The GC–MS profiles were analyzed by A Bruker Gas Chromatograph 450-GC and TQ mass Spectrometer 320-MS.

The volatile compounds from geminated rice were analyzed by gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (Agilent 7890 A GC-7000 Mass Triple Quad) equipped with a capillary column (DB-WAX, 60 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm, J&W Scientific, Folsom, CA) and a quadrupole mass detector with electron ionization mode (m/z 40–250, 70 eV). The shoots (4.0 g) in capped headspace vial were pre-heated at 50°C for 10 min. The volatile compounds were extracted for 20 min by SPME fiber (50/30 μm DVB/CAR/PDMS, SUPELCO, PA) in GC injector port at 230°C, carried out by helium gas (0.8 mL/min). The volatile compounds were identified by an Agilent MassHunter Qualitative Analysis B.04.00 software and then compared mass spectra with NIST mass spectral libraries (National Institute of Standards, 2011 version). The content of volatile compounds was calculated from the peak area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of elephant manure derived‑carbon dots (ECDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Elephant manure was used as a carbon precursor for carbon dot synthesis since it contains cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin [21]. ECDs were green synthesized through a bottom-up approach by the hydrothermal method at 200°C due to their crystallization at high temperature and pressure in an aqueous solution (Scheme 1 ) [28]. The ECDs solution was orange-brown and exhibited blue fluorescence emission.

As shown in Fig. 1a, functional groups of as-prepared ECDs were examined by FTIR. The FTIR spectra of ECDs showed intense peaks at 3214 cm−1 (O-H/N–H stretching), 2930 cm−1 (C–H stretching), 1681 cm−1 (CC stretching), 1388 cm−1 (O–H bending), 1060 cm−1 (C–O stretching), and 780 cm−1 (C–H bending), indicating that the functional groups of ECDs are hydroxyl groups and sp2/sp3 hybridized‑carbon atoms [29,30]. Furthermore, other peaks displayed at 1285 cm−1 (C–N stretching) and 1594 cm−1(N–H stretching) attributed to the presence of amino group (−NH2) [31,32]. ECDs-24 h also consisted of carboxylic acids due to the C–O stretching peak at 1762 cm−1. From the FTIR spectra, the period of time used for the synthesis process influenced the structure of ECDs because increasing the synthesis time can cause hydrolysis of the precursor molecules [33]. The zeta potential of ECDs was also examined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique (Fig. 1b). The obtained data of ECDs-6 h, ECDs-12 h, and ECDs-24 h were − 30.89 ± 0.67 mV, −20.12 ± 0.84 mV, and − 19.00 ± 1.03 mV, respectively. The negative values of zeta potential resulted from the deprotonation of the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [34]. The electric charge is directly proportional to the length of time used for synthesis, resulting in different physical properties of ECDs [35].

The surface states and elemental composition of ECDs-12 h have also been elucidated with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectrum of ECDs-12 h shows three peaks at 285, 400, and 532 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1c) [36]. According to the XPS analysis, the elemental composition of ECDs-12 h was carbon (C1s, 58.3%), oxygen (O1s, 24.9%), and nitrogen (N1s, 8.4%). The high-resolution spectra of C1s show the binding energies of 284.9, 286.3, and 288.1 eV, according to the C=C/C–C, C–O/C–N, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 1d) [37]. The high-resolution spectra of O1s showed the binding energies of 530.9, 531.7, 532.8, and 534.0 eV, derived from the CO, C–O–C, O–C=O, and C–OH bonds, respectively (Fig. 1e) [29]. The high-resolution spectra of N1s show the binding energies of 400.0 and 401.5 eV, derived from the N–H and C–N–C bonds, respectively (Fig. 1f) [38]. The data obtained from XPS agreed well with those from XPS owing to the presence of hydroxyl (-OH), carboxylic (-COOH), and amino (−NH2) groups. These results suggested that the time for synthesis process affects chemical composition, particle size, and surface functional groups of ECDs [35].

The morphology and average diameter of ECDs-12 h were characterized by TEM (Fig. 2 ). The TEM images showed that ECDs-12 h have a uniform spherical shape and well dispersion. The average diameter from the size distribution histogram of ECDs was 7.8 ± 1.2 nm from the range of 5–10 nm (Fig. 2b). In addition, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns show that the broad diffraction peak of the (002) plane of graphite was centered at 2θ = 20.77°, which be classified as the amorphous structure of the ECDs-12 h (Fig. 2c) [39,40].

The optical properties of ECDs were studied by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 3 ). The yellow-brown ECDs solution showed intense absorbance at 274–282 and 327 nm due to the characteristic π → π⁎ transition of sp2-hybridized carbon framework and n → π⁎ transition of the carbonyl groups, respectively [41]. The ECDs solution exhibited blue fluorescence emission under ultraviolet (365 nm) due to the electron-hole pair energetic band gap of sp2-hybridized carbon with the carbonyl and hydroxyl moieties [42]. The maximum fluorescence emission was observed at 443 nm by excitation at 360 nm. Tunable fluorescence enhancement was observed in a bathochromic shift from 439 to 535 nm with the excitation wavelength range of 240–500 nm. In addition, the quantum yields (Φ) of ECDs-6 h, ECDs-12 h, and ECDs-24 h were 9.6 ± 0.3%, 20.2 ± 0.2%, and 11.5 ± 0.2%, respectively (Fig. S1). These results suggested that the quantum yield of ECDs increased with the increasing time of synthesis and then decreased at the prolonged time of synthesis since more carbon nucleus was generated, leading to higher fluorescence emission [43]. As the time of synthesis further increased, there was a gradual increase in both growth rate and size of carbon nucleus, resulting in the reduction of fluorescence intensity [43]. Therefore, ECDs-12 h with the highest quantum yield was selected as the fluorescent sensor and further used for metal ion detection.

The optical stability and photo-bleaching of ECDs were also studied under UV irradiation at 365 nm and visible light (Fig. 4 ). The relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of ECDs-12 h under UV irradiation was observed at about 96%, which was more stable than ECDs-6 h (90%) and ECDs-24 h (91%). Moreover, the F/F0 of ECDs-12 h (88%) was higher than ECDs-6 h (83%) and EDCs-24 h (86%) under visible light for 14 days without aggregation. These results indicated that ECDs-12 h exhibited higher photostability and tolerance to photo-bleaching than other EDCs. Besides, the stability of ECDs in the presence of different high salt concentrations (0–2 M) was also studied to determine the effect of ionic strength on fluorescence intensity. No fluorescence change of ECDs-12 h was observed and greater than ECDs-6 h (98%) and ECDs-24 h (96%), indicating that ECDs-12 h has excellent stability even under a high ionic strength medium.

Another important property of CDs is the use for plant promotion. The antioxidant activity of CDs was reported to improve the stress tolerance of plants in adverse growth environments [6]. The antioxidant capabilities of ECDs are closely associated with their ability to donate hydrogen or electron atoms to the DPPH free radical [44]. The antioxidant activity of ECDs was elucidated via the radical scavenging activity of 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazy (DPPH) (Table 1 ). ECDs-12 h exhibited antioxidant activity higher than ECDs-6 h and ECDs-24 h, respectively. Total phenolic contents of ECDs were also determined using Fotin-Ciocalteu method from the standard curve of gallic acid (GAE) with y = 0.0302× + 0.0332, R
2 = 0.9963. The phenolic composition of ECDs-12 h was 8.73 ± 0.10 mg GAE/mg ECDs, which was greater than those of ECDs-6 h and ECDs-24 h, respectively. ECDs-12 h contained the highest total phenolic content, leading to the highest antioxidant activity, as the free hydroxyl group of phenolic derivatives displayed redox properties, corresponding to the previous reports [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of ECDs-12 h for metal ion sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the highest quantum yield, high photostability, high phenolic content, and no photo-bleaching of ECDs-12 h, the selectivity of ECDs-12 h was examined with various metal ions, such as Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, As3+, As5+, Mn2+ and Cd2+ in deionized water (Fig. 5a). The fluorescence intensity of ECDs-12 h significantly decreased in the presence of Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+, whereas other metal ions showed no change in fluorescence. The fluorescence quenching of ECDs-12 h was observed by Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+, which could be due to the stronger binding affinity of these ions with surface functional groups, including phenolic hydroxyl or amine groups [46,47]. However, the fluorescence intensity of ECDs-12 h showed a slight decrease upon adding Pb2+ and Cu2+ ions due to carboxyl and amine groups on the surface of ECDs-12 h, according to the previous reports [48,49]. In the competitive experiment, fluorescence quenching of ECDs-12 h was observed in the presence of Hg2+ ions and other interfering metal ions, indicating that fluorescence quenching was induced by Hg2+ ions without an interference of other metal ions. Furthermore, the optical stability of ECDs-12 h was studied under different pH buffers. The F/F0 of ECDs-12 h was highly observed in the pH range of 5–7, but it decreased in the high acidic solutions because the proton charge of the surface functional group is destroyed or surface charge exchange occurs [50]. The degree of ECDs-12 h deprotonation increased as the pH value increased from 7 to 13, which diminished the fluorescence intensity [51]. The fluorescence property of ECDs-12 h in phosphate buffer was similar to that in distilled water. Therefore, distilled water was chosen for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of ECDs-12 h for Hg2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ detections
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensitivity of ECDs-12 h is an important parameter in demonstrating the detection efficiency for Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+. Fluorescence emission titration spectra of ECDs-12 h (0.5 mg/mL) were investigated in the presence of Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ under optimum conditions (Fig. 6 ). Fluorescence quenching of ECDs-12 h at 443 nm decreased gradually with increasing concentrations of Hg2+ (0–30 mM), Fe2+ (0–9 mM), and Fe3+ (0–8 mM). Linear correlation curves of fluorescence intensity and concentrations of Hg2+ (0.03–0.5 mM, R
2 = 0.9978), Fe2+ (0.02–3.0 mM, R
2 = 0.9925) and Fe3+ (0.01–0.6 mM, R
2 = 0.9978) were established and used to calculate the limit of detection (LOD). The LODs were 1.03 μM for Hg2+, 18.52 μM for Fe2+, and 0.65 μM for Fe3+ (S/N = 3) according to three standard deviations. The LOD for Hg2+ detection was lower than the previous literature (Table 2 ). The quenching efficiency of ECDs-12 h was elucidated by the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (K
sv ) from the slope of F0/F vs the concentrations of metal ions, including Hg2+ (0–1 mM, R
2 = 0.9888), Fe2+ (0–8 mM, R
2 = 0.9843), and Fe3+ (0–8 mM, R
2 = 0.9864). The K
sv constants for Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ were calculated to be 3.1 × 103, 8.2 × 102, and 2.1 × 103 L mol−1, respectively. These results suggested that the fluorescence quenching of ECDs-12 h by Hg2+ was more effective than Fe3+ and Fe2+, respectively. Time-dependent plots of the ECDs-12 h in the presence of Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ were examined under optimal conditions (Fig. 7 ). The fluorescence emission of ECDs-12 h was extinguished immediately 1 min after adding Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+, indicating that ECDs-12 h could become a real-time monitoring sensor for Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible quenching mechanism for metal ion sensing
ce:section.ce:para: As a result, the fluorescence intensity of ECDs-12 h decreased in the presence of Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+, the possible quenching mechanism of ECDs-12 h against these metal ions was investigated as shown in Fig. 8 . The ECDs-12 h solution with Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+showed no photoluminescence under UV illumination (365 nm), indicating that photoluminescence fluorescence resonant energy transfer (FRET) is not a reliable mechanism for metal ion detection [55]. A solution of ECDs-12 h (0.1 mg/mL) in the presence of Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+(100 μM) showed a slight change in the UV–Vis absorption spectra in the range of 250–500 nm. The UV absorption spectra of metal ions as fluorescence quencher overlapped with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of ECDs-12 h. Therefore, the fluorescence reduction process for these metal ions occurred via the intrinsic filter effect (IFE) [56]. Fluorescence emission of ECDs-12 h was quenched via the non-radiative electron transfer method in the excitation state to the d-orbital of the metal ions. As the functional group of ECDs-12 h consists of hydroxyl and amine groups, the Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ ions may chelate to the moiety, leading to the binding and fluorescence quenching of the ECDs-12 h [55,57–60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based strip of CDs-SH for metal ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: The ECDs-12 h coated paper strips were prepared for metal ion sensing, as shown in Fig. 9 . The ECDs-12 h coated paper strip showed blue fluorescence emission under UV light after immersion in metal ion solutions including Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, As2+, As2+, Mn2+ and Cd2+. While the paper strip was immersed in the Hg2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ solutions, no fluorescence was observed under UV light. However, Pb2+ and Cu2+ slightly affected the fluorescence emission of the paper-coated ECDs-12 h compared with ECDs-12 h paper-coated strips. These results indicate that paper-coated ECDs-12 h can detect these metal ions, including Hg2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the fluorescence sensor of ECDs-12 h for Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ ions in drinking water, Table 3 shows the results of the fluorescence sensor for detecting Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ ions at concentrations of 30 and 50 μM. The recovery of ECDs-12 h was found in the standard range (% recovery = 80–110), indicating that ECDs-12 h had good metal ion detection performance.

The effects of ECDs-12 h on the growth rate and 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline (2-AP) content of rice were examined (Fig. 10 ). The rice seeds were treated at different concentrations of ECDs-12 h compared to the distilled water as the control. The shoot length of ECDs-12 h at 10 and 50 mg/mL treated rice seeds was enhanced by 16.8%, which was greater than that of control seeds. Since Khao Gor Kor 43 was classified as a fragrant rice cultivar, 2AP has been reported as the major contributor to the characteristic aroma compounds of fragrant rice cultivars [61]. The accumulation of 2AP content in the rice shoots with ECDs-12 h pretreatment was also determined. The ECDs-12 h treated-rice shoots at 100 mg/mL increased 5.1% and showed different 2AP content from other conditions and control. Due to the hydrophilic surface groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amine groups, ECDs were able to interact the water molecule and then transport them into the rice seeds, which is beneficial for seed germination, and root development [62]. The results also suggested that ECDs-12 h could increase the growth of rice plants, corresponding to the previous report [62].

The volatile compounds of the ECDs-12 h-treated rice at the concentrations of 10, 50, and 100 mg/L were identified by using GC–MS analysis (Table 4 ). GC–MS analysis revealed the presence of 50 volatile compounds in the rice-pretreated ECDs-12 h. For the rice with the incubation of ECDs-12 h (10–100 mg/L), several volatile compounds such as methyl pyrazine, 5-methyl-2-furanmethanol, 2,4-dimethyl-2-oxazoline-4-methanol, 2-acetylpyrrole, phenol, pentadecanal, and 2-n-hexylcyclopentanone have been reported for biological activities related to human health. For example, 5-methyl-2-furanmethanol has been reported in Gunnera perpensa with anticancer, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities [63]. Methyl pyrazine and 2,4-dimethyl-2-oxazoline-4-methanol analyzed from ethanolic extracts of T. porrifolius and P. cognatum showed antioxidant and antimicrobial activities, as well as α-amylase and α-glucosidase inhibition activities [64]. Pentadecanal, the essential oil identified in S. erianthum and S. macranthum, showed antimicrobial activity [65]. Moreover, the results indicated that ECDs-12 h could promote the content of aroma derivatives in fragrant rice, such as 2AP, 2-acetylpyrrole, and methyl pyrazine (Fig. 10 and Table 4). 2-Acetyl pyrrole, as an aroma compound, was identified in many plants [66]. Methyl pyrazine was reported as an active aroma compound, and it has the odor quality toasty [66]. 2AP can be chemically synthesized using 2-acetylpyrrole as the first precursor [67]. Therefore, ECDs-12 h could enhance the contribution of aroma compounds and biological activity in fragrant rice.

The blue fluorescent ECDs were easily prepared from elephant manure as biomass via one-pot synthesis of the hydrothermal method. Due to the surface functional groups, which included amino and hydroxyl moieties, the ECDs showed a monodispersed spherical form. The ECDs demonstrated strong absorption peaks in the ultraviolet (UV) range at wavelengths of 274–282 nm, which can be attributed to a π → π* transition. Additionally, these ECDs exhibited fluorescence emission that depended on the excitation, and they did not undergo photobleaching when exposed to UV light. Furthermore, they showed great photostability when exposed to visible light and high concentrations of salt. ECDs-12 h as a “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ ions had detection limits of 1.03, 18.52, and 0.65 μM, respectively. The fluorescence quenching for these ions occurred through non-radiative electron transfer of ECDs-12 h, while Fe3+ sensing was suggested via the inner filter effect (IFE) process. ECDs-12 h was further applied to detect Hg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ in drinking water with a good recovery of 83%–107%. The blue fluorescence emission of paper-coated ECDs-12 h was not detected in the presence of these ions compared to other metal ions. ECDs showed total phenolic content relating to the DPPH radical scavenging activity. ECDs-12 h could increase the growth of rice plant and enhance the production of aroma compounds and biological activity in fragrant rice. From these results, the as-synthesized CDs showed their potential applications in various fields of metal ion sensing, antioxidant activity, plant promotion, and biological activity in fragrant rice.
